intro:
Rome, the capital of the Roman Empire on the Tiber in central Italy, was the political center of the world in which the New Testament was written and, in prophetic symbol, the archetype of the world-power arrayed against the people of God. Founded by tradition in 753 BC and grown by the first century AD into a metropolis of perhaps a million souls, Rome ruled an empire stretching from Britain to the Euphrates, holding the Pax Romana by which roads, commerce, and a common Greek tongue providentially prepared the way for the spread of the gospel.

For the early church, Rome was both a goal and a symbol. Paul, the apostle to the Gentiles, longed to preach there: "I am eager to preach the gospel to you also who are in Rome" (Romans 1:15), intending it as a base for further mission to Spain (Romans 15:24). His longest and most theological letter, Romans, was written to the believers there. In Jerusalem the risen Lord stood by him and said, "as you have testified about me in Jerusalem, so you must testify also in Rome" (Acts 23:11). Having appealed to Caesar (Acts 25:11), Paul reached the city at last as a prisoner, and the book of Acts closes with him there proclaiming the kingdom of God "with all boldness and without hindrance" (Acts 28:30–31) — a fitting end to the gospel's journey "to the end of the earth" (Acts 1:8).

A large Jewish community had lived in Rome since the second century BC. Luke notes that the Emperor Claudius had expelled the Jews, which is why Paul met Aquila and Priscilla, lately come from Italy, in Corinth (Acts 18:2). The gospel reached Rome early — visitors from Rome had been present at Pentecost (Acts 2:10) — and a vigorous church existed there by the 50s, whose faith Paul says was "proclaimed in all the world" (Romans 1:8). Under Nero, following the great fire of AD 64, that church suffered fierce persecution; ancient tradition places the martyrdoms of both Peter and Paul in Rome during this period. Peter likely writes from there under the veiled name "Babylon" (1 Peter 5:13).

This identification carries into the Bible's last book, where Rome becomes the great symbolic Babylon: "the woman... seated on seven mountains" (Revelation 17:9) — the famed seven hills of Rome — "drunk with the blood of the saints" (Revelation 17:6), whose luxury, idolatry, and persecution typify every human empire that exalts itself against the Lamb. Revelation's announcement, "Fallen, fallen is Babylon the great" (Revelation 18:2), assures the church that the city of man, however mighty, must yield before the eternal city of God.

link_tables.title: Places

cells: Rome
The imperial capital; Paul preached here unhindered to the end of Acts (Acts 28:30–31)
Jerusalem
Under Roman rule; where Christ was crucified and Paul arrested (Acts 21:33)
Caesarea
Roman provincial seat where Paul was held and appealed to Caesar (Acts 25:11)
Corinth
Capital of Roman Achaia; Paul tried before the proconsul Gallio (Acts 18:12)
Ephesus
Chief city of Roman Asia; long center of Paul’s mission (Acts 19:10)
Philippi
A Roman colony where Paul invoked his citizenship (Acts 16:12)
Athens
Where Paul reasoned with the philosophers at the Areopagus (Acts 17:22)
Puteoli
Italian port where Paul landed on the way to Rome (Acts 28:13)
Forum of Appius
On the road to Rome, where believers came out to meet Paul (Acts 28:15)

link_tables.note: Click any name to open its article. Markers for these places and the event below appear on the map above.

cells: The Crucifixion
Under Roman rule, by Pilate’s sentence, Christ was crucified for the world (Mark 15:15)
Augustus
Caesar whose decree sent Joseph to Bethlehem for Jesus’ birth (Luke 2:1)
Tiberius
Emperor in whose reign John the Baptist and Jesus ministered (Luke 3:1)
Pontius Pilate
Roman governor who condemned Jesus to be crucified (John 19:16)
Claudius
Emperor who expelled the Jews from Rome (Acts 18:2)
Nero
The Caesar to whom Paul appealed; first great persecutor of the church (Acts 25:11)
Felix
Governor who kept Paul bound at Caesarea two years (Acts 24:27)
Festus
Successor who referred Paul’s appeal to Caesar (Acts 25:12)
Agrippa
King before whom Paul made his defense (Acts 26:1)
Gallio
Proconsul of Achaia who dismissed the charge against Paul (Acts 18:14–16)
Julius
Centurion who guarded Paul on the voyage to Rome (Acts 27:1)
Paul
Apostle who carried the gospel to Rome itself, a Roman citizen (Acts 22:25–28)
Peter
Wrote from ‘Babylon’ (Rome); tradition marks his martyrdom there (1 Pet 5:13)
Aquila
Jewish believer expelled from Rome under Claudius (Acts 18:2)
Priscilla
Aquila’s wife and fellow worker with Paul (Rom 16:3)
